nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef flats, rocky substrates, and reef rubble where film and turf algae are abundant.

decorPreferences: established live rock with film algae

diet.notes: Voracious grazer of film algae, diatoms, cyanobacteria, and nuisance hair algae. In sparsely-algaed tanks, supplement with blanched spinach, nori sheets, or vegetable-based pellet foods to prevent starvation.

summary: A small, peaceful Caribbean reef snail prized as a clean-up-crew workhorse. Generally considered one of the most effective consumers of film algae, diatoms, and nuisance hair algae in saltwater aquariums.
careNotes: Astraea Snail is the long-standing trade name for Lithopoma tectum (older synonym Astraea tecta), a small olive-green pyramid-shelled turbinid native to the Caribbean and tropical Western Atlantic. It is commonly considered a beginner-friendly reef cleanup crew member: smaller and less bulldozer-like than true Turbo snails, so it knocks over corals and frags less often, while still grazing efficiently on film algae, diatoms, cyanobacteria, and short hair algae. CRITICAL HUSBANDRY NOTE: Astraea Snails are notoriously poor at righting themselves once knocked upside-down, whether by tankmates, water flow, or their own awkward climbing. A flipped Astraea will slowly starve and die over a few days if not turned right-side-up by the aquarist. Daily visual checks and prompt manual righting are essential — this is the single biggest cause of premature death in this species. Like all marine invertebrates, they are highly copper-sensitive and cannot be exposed to copper-based medications; they also decline rapidly in tanks with elevated nitrates or unstable salinity. Drip-acclimate slowly over 1-2 hours to avoid osmotic shock. Generally considered fully reef-safe with corals, but predatory tankmates (pufferfish, triggerfish, large wrasses, predatory hermits, and stone or emerald crabs) will attack them. Stock at roughly 1 per 4-8 liters depending on algae load. Note: the current accepted taxonomy is Lithopoma tectum (Turbinidae); Astraea tecta remains the most common trade name and appears in nearly all hobby-source product pages.
breedingNotes: Has a planktonic larval phase typical of marine turbinid snails; spawning is broadcast and the veliger larvae require open-water plankton-feeding conditions to develop. Captive breeding is not practical in home reef aquaria and is not documented as a hobby achievement.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, water parameters (temp 24-25.5C, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.025-1.026, dKH 8-9.5), reef-safe and care-level confirmation, and Caribbean origin.

additional.name: WoRMS - Lithopoma tectum
additional.notes: Confirmed accepted taxonomy: Lithopoma tectum (Lightfoot, 1786), family Turbinidae, order Trochida. Astraea tecta is listed as a synonym.

additional.name: Aquarium Specialty - The Astraea Snail (Astraea tecta)
additional.notes: Third source for size (~1-1.5 in / up to ~4 cm), lifespan (2-3 years), and the flip-and-cannot-right warning.
